skill.name: when-verifying-quality-use-verification-quality
skill.description: Guides comprehensive release quality checks using static analysis, automated tests, integration validation, certification gates, and reporting.
skill.summary: Verify release quality with evidence-based gates and structured reports.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical heuristic because Markdown examples combine commands, local network checks, filesystem reads, and a private-key signing example. Review found no prompt injection, no data exfiltration, and no confirmed malicious intent; the main risk is that the skill guides agents to run many local quality and CI commands.

medium_findings.title: Extensive Local Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill contains many Markdown examples that instruct agents to run npm, npx, SonarQube, ESLint, TypeScript, Cypress, Playwright, and CI commands. This is legitimate for quality verification, but it can execute project scripts and third-party tools in the user workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in fenced documentation examples and match the stated quality-verification purpose. Risk remains because following the skill can run arbitrary project-defined npm scripts and external tools.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Artifact Creation and Reading
medium_findings.description: The workflow directs agents to create audit reports, read generated JSON files, and post CI summaries. These operations are expected, but they require workspace file access and may process sensitive build details.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are visible in example bash and JavaScript blocks. They appear scoped to quality artifacts, but they should be run only in trusted repositories.

medium_findings.title: Local Service and Tool Network Checks
medium_findings.description: The process documentation includes curl checks for local PostgreSQL and RabbitMQ endpoints. The examples use localhost and do not show data exfiltration, but they still represent network activity during validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The detected URLs are localhost checks in an integration validation section. This is likely benign, but it is still a network behavior users should understand.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto and C2 Keyword Matches Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner matched weak-crypto and C2-like tokens in diagram colors, SOC2 compliance text, and certification signing examples. No malicious command-and-control behavior or weak cryptographic storage logic was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted line review showed Graphviz styling and compliance labels, not executable malicious logic. The certificate-signing example uses sha256 rather than a weak digest.

low_findings.title: Example Private Key Reference Requires Operator Control
low_findings.description: The certification example signs an approval artifact with private-key.pem. This is not credential theft, but users must provide and protect the key outside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The key path appears only in a signing example for release certification. It is legitimate but sensitive enough to document as a caution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Extensive Local Command Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many Markdown examples that instruct agents to run npm, npx, SonarQube, ESLint, TypeScript, Cypress, Playwright, and CI commands. This is legitimate for quality verification, but it can execute project scripts and third-party tools in the user workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in fenced documentation examples and match the stated quality-verification purpose. Risk remains because following the skill can run arbitrary project-defined npm scripts and external tools.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Artifact Creation and Reading
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow directs agents to create audit reports, read generated JSON files, and post CI summaries. These operations are expected, but they require workspace file access and may process sensitive build details.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are visible in example bash and JavaScript blocks. They appear scoped to quality artifacts, but they should be run only in trusted repositories.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Service and Tool Network Checks
dangerous_patterns.description: The process documentation includes curl checks for local PostgreSQL and RabbitMQ endpoints. The examples use localhost and do not show data exfiltration, but they still represent network activity during validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The detected URLs are localhost checks in an integration validation section. This is likely benign, but it is still a network behavior users should understand.

content.user_title: Verify Release Quality with Evidence Gates
content.value_statement: Release decisions often rely on scattered test results and manual judgment. This skill organizes static analysis, testing, integration checks, and certification into one evidence-based workflow.
actual_capabilities: Defines a five-phase quality verification workflow for release readiness.
Specifies static analysis checks for complexity, duplication, and maintainability.
Guides unit, integration, end-to-end, and coverage validation steps.
Documents certification scoring with weighted quality gates.
Outlines report generation for Markdown, HTML, PDF, and summary artifacts.
Provides CI examples for enforcing certification thresholds.
limitations: Requires project-specific npm scripts and tools to exist before commands can run.
Does not include executable implementation files for the verification pipeline.
Example metrics are illustrative and must be replaced with real project data.
Private signing keys and CI credentials must be managed outside the skill.

use_cases.title: Release Gate Review
use_cases.description: Run a structured quality review before approving a production release.

use_cases.title: CI Quality Enforcement
use_cases.description: Add certification score checks and report uploads to a pull request workflow.

use_cases.title: Audit Evidence Preparation
use_cases.description: Collect quality metrics, compliance checks, and sign-off details for stakeholders.

prompt_templates.title: Run Basic Verification
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a normal quality check.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to verify quality for the current project. Report the commands you would run and the expected artifacts.

prompt_templates.title: Check Release Readiness
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate release review with existing reports.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my static analysis, test, and integration reports. Calculate whether the release meets the certification gates.

prompt_templates.title: Build CI Quality Gates
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CI workflow that runs the quality verification phases and blocks merges below the certification threshold.

prompt_templates.title: Create Audit Package
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a stakeholder quality certification package with metrics, risks, sign-off status, and required follow-up actions.

output_examples.input: Verify whether this build is ready for release.
output_examples.output: A phase-by-phase quality summary with certification score, failed gates, release status, and recommended remediation steps.

output_examples.input: Add quality verification to our pull request workflow.
output_examples.output: A CI plan that runs tests, checks coverage, reads the certification score, uploads reports, and blocks weak builds.

output_examples.input: Prepare a quality report for stakeholders.
output_examples.output: An executive summary with key metrics, compliance status, known issues, and a clear approval recommendation.

best_practices: Run commands in a clean branch or CI environment before trusting generated reports.
Replace sample thresholds and scores with project-approved quality gates.
Review external tools and credentials before enabling automated execution.
anti_patterns: Do not treat the sample certification scores as real project evidence.
Do not run project scripts from untrusted repositories without inspection.
Do not store private signing keys or CI secrets in generated reports.

faq.question: Does this skill run tests by itself?
faq.answer: It provides a workflow and command examples. The target project must supply the actual scripts and tools.

faq.question: Can it replace a QA engineer?
faq.answer: No. It organizes evidence and gates, but humans still review exceptions, risk, and release context.

faq.question: Which tools does it mention?
faq.answer: It references SonarQube, ESLint, TypeScript, Jest, Cypress, Playwright, Pact, Postman, and CI workflows.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use it with normal command review. It may run local scripts and read generated reports in the workspace.

faq.question: Does it include real implementation code?
faq.answer: No. It is mainly Markdown guidance with examples for building a quality verification process.

faq.question: Can the thresholds be changed?
faq.answer: Yes. The thresholds are examples and should match the project risk profile and release policy.
